column1: 12FACTOR-01-CODEBASE|2|Maintain one codebase per app in version control; deploy it to many environments without branching.
12FACTOR-02-DEPENDENCIES|2|Explicitly declare and isolate all dependencies; never rely on implicit system-wide packages.
12FACTOR-03-CONFIG|2|Store all config in environment variables; never hardcode credentials or environment-specific values in code.
12FACTOR-04-BACKING-SERVICES|2|Treat all backing services as attached resources configurable via URL; swap them without code changes.
12FACTOR-05-BUILD-RELEASE-RUN|2|Strictly separate build, release, and run stages; every release must be immutable and uniquely identified.
12FACTOR-06-PROCESSES|2|Run stateless, share-nothing processes; store all persistent state in external backing services.
12FACTOR-07-PORT-BINDING|2|Export services by binding to a port using an embedded server; never rely on external application servers.
12FACTOR-08-CONCURRENCY|2|Scale out by running more stateless process instances; assign distinct workloads to distinct process types.
12FACTOR-09-DISPOSABILITY|2|Design processes to start quickly, shut down gracefully on SIGTERM, and survive sudden death safely.
12FACTOR-10-DEV-PROD-PARITY|2|Keep development, staging, and production environments as similar as possible to prevent environment-specific bugs.
12FACTOR-11-LOGS|2|Write all logs to stdout as an unbuffered event stream; let the platform handle routing and storage.
12FACTOR-12-ADMIN-PROCESSES|2|Run admin tasks as one-off processes using the same code, environment, and config as the running app.
A11Y-ALT-TEXT|2|Provide meaningful alt text for every informative image; use empty alt="" for decorative images.
A11Y-COLOR-CONTRAST|2|Ensure normal text meets 4.5:1 contrast ratio and large text meets 3:1 against its background.
A11Y-KEYBOARD-NAVIGATION|2|Ensure every interactive element is reachable, activatable, and escapable using keyboard alone.
A11Y-SEMANTIC-HTML|2|Use native HTML elements for their semantic meaning; never replace buttons and links with generic divs.
ARCH-ACTIVE-RECORD|2|Use Active Record only for simple CRUD domains where the data model and domain model are closely aligned.
ARCH-ANTI-CORRUPTION-LAYER|2|Introduce a translation layer at every external integration boundary to protect your domain model from leakage.
ARCH-API-GATEWAY|2|Route all external traffic through a single API gateway that enforces authentication, rate limiting, and routing.
ARCH-ARCHITECTURE-AS-CODE|1|Define the architecture model in version-controlled code so it stays in sync with the system it describes.
ARCH-BACKENDS-FOR-FRONTENDS|2|Create a dedicated backend service per client type; never build one general-purpose API for all clients.
ARCH-BULKHEAD|2|Isolate subsystem resources into separate pools so one failure cannot exhaust resources needed by others.
ARCH-CONWAYS-LAW|2|Align team structure with desired system architecture; design teams to produce the communication boundaries you want.
ARCH-DATA-MAPPER|2|Use a dedicated mapper to transfer data between domain objects and the database, keeping both fully independent.
ARCH-DATABASE-PER-SERVICE|2|Each microservice must own its data exclusively; no other service may bypass its API to access its database.
ARCH-DECISION-RECORDS|1|Capture every significant architectural decision as an ADR recording context, decision, and consequences.
ARCH-DESIGN-FOR-FAILURE|2|Assume every component will fail; design for graceful degradation rather than cascading outage.
ARCH-HEXAGONAL|2|Keep the application core free of infrastructure dependencies by exposing ports implemented by swappable adapters.
ARCH-LAYERED|2|Organize code into layers where each layer depends only on the layer directly below it; never skip layers.
ARCH-MICROKERNEL|2|Separate a minimal stable core from plugin modules that extend it only through the published plugin API.
ARCH-MVC|1|Separate domain state, rendering, and user-input handling into Model, View, and Controller with no cross-responsibility.
ARCH-OBSERVABILITY-BY-DESIGN|1|Design every system to expose structured logs, metrics, and distributed traces from the outset, not as an afterthought.
ARCH-REPLACEABILITY|2|Design every component to be replaceable without requiring changes to its neighbors; prefer explicit versioned interfaces.
ARCH-SAGA|2|Coordinate distributed transactions as a sequence of local transactions with compensating steps on failure.
ARCH-SERVICE-BOUNDARIES|2|Own data exclusively within each service and communicate across boundaries only through versioned explicit contracts.
ARCH-SERVICE-LAYER|2|Define application use cases in a service layer that coordinates domain objects and enforces transactional boundaries.
ARCH-SIDECAR|2|Deploy cross-cutting operational concerns as independent co-located sidecar processes, not as in-process libraries.
ARCH-STATELESS-FIRST|2|Design service instances to hold no state beyond a single request; store all session state in an external store.
ARCH-STRANGLER-FIG|2|Incrementally replace legacy systems by routing traffic to a new implementation piece by piece; never big-bang rewrite.
ARCH-TRANSACTION-SCRIPT|2|Use Transaction Script only for simple low-complexity domains; avoid it as business logic grows and duplicates.
ARCH-UNIT-OF-WORK|2|Track all domain changes in a Unit of Work and commit them in one coordinated database transaction at completion.
CD-BLUE-GREEN-DEPLOYMENT|2|Deploy to an idle environment first, run smoke tests, then switch traffic; keep the previous environment for instant rollback.
CD-BUILD-ONCE-DEPLOY-MANY|1|Build the artifact once and promote that same immutable artifact through every environment.
CD-CANARY-RELEASE|2|Route a small fraction of traffic to the new version and roll back automatically if metrics degrade.
CD-DEPLOY-ON-EVERY-COMMIT|2|Automate the pipeline so any green commit can be deployed to production without manual intervention.
CD-DEPLOYMENT-SMOKE-TESTS|2|Run automated smoke tests after every deployment to verify core functionality before routing real traffic.
CD-FAST-FEEDBACK-LOOPS|1|Structure the pipeline to return a pass or fail signal to the developer within ten minutes.
CD-FEATURE-FLAGS|2|Decouple deployment from release by hiding incomplete features behind runtime flags, not branches.
CD-GITOPS|2|Declare all infrastructure state in git and let an automated operator continuously reconcile the live environment.
CD-KEEP-BUILD-GREEN|1|Treat a failing main branch build as the team's highest priority; nothing ships until it passes.
CD-PIPELINE-AS-CODE|1|Commit every CI/CD pipeline step to version control and review it like application code.
CD-SEMANTIC-VERSIONING|1|Use MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH version numbers to communicate the scope and safety of changes to consumers.
CD-TRUNK-BASED-DEVELOPMENT|1|Integrate to the main branch at least daily; use feature flags to hide incomplete work instead of branches.
CLEAN-ARCH-ARCHITECTURAL-BOUNDARIES|2|Draw explicit boundaries between components and enforce all cross-boundary communication through well-defined interfaces.
CLEAN-ARCH-DEPENDENCY-RULE|2|Source code dependencies must always point inward toward business rules, never outward toward frameworks or details.
CLEAN-ARCH-KEEP-OPTIONS-OPEN|2|Defer binding to specific frameworks and databases by keeping them behind interfaces as long as possible.
CLEAN-ARCH-SEPARATE-BUSINESS-RULES|2|Keep business logic in framework-free code that can be tested without starting a web server or database.
CODE-API-BACKWARD-COMPATIBILITY|2|Only add to published APIs; never remove, rename, or change fields without introducing a new version.
CODE-API-CONDITIONAL-REQUESTS|2|Require `If-Match` on concurrent writes and honour `If-None-Match` on GETs to prevent lost updates and wasted bandwidth.
CODE-API-CONTENT-NEGOTIATION|2|Inspect the `Accept` header and respond with the negotiated media type, returning 406 if none is supported.
CODE-API-GRPC-PROTOBUF|2|Define gRPC contracts in `.proto` files and never reuse a field number after it has been retired.
CODE-API-HATEOAS|2|Include hypermedia links in every response so clients discover available actions rather than hardcoding URIs.
CODE-API-HTTP-CACHING|2|Set explicit `Cache-Control` and validation headers on every GET response; never rely on intermediary defaults.
CODE-API-HTTP-STATUS-CODES|2|Use HTTP status codes precisely to communicate outcomes; never return 200 for errors.
CODE-API-PAGINATION|2|Paginate every collection endpoint with a server-enforced maximum page size; never return unbounded results.
CODE-API-PROBLEM-DETAILS|2|Return RFC 7807 Problem Details for all error responses; never expose stack traces or internal details.
CODE-API-RATE-LIMITING|2|Enforce rate limits on all APIs and return 429 with `Retry-After` when clients exceed their quota.
CODE-API-RESOURCE-NOUNS|2|Identify resources with noun URIs and let HTTP methods express the action, never verbs in the path.
CODE-API-STANDARD-HTTP-METHODS|2|Honor HTTP method semantics — GET must be safe, PUT and DELETE must be idempotent.
CODE-API-VERSIONING|2|Version every API explicitly and maintain old versions through a documented deprecation period before removal.
CODE-AR-ASYNC-MESSAGING|2|Prefer asynchronous messaging over synchronous calls whenever the sender does not need an immediate response.
CODE-AR-COMPOSABLE-MODULES|2|Organize infrastructure into small, focused, independently manageable modules with clear inputs and outputs.
CODE-AR-IMMUTABLE-INFRASTRUCTURE|2|Replace infrastructure components by building new immutable instances; never modify running instances in place.
CODE-AR-INFRASTRUCTURE-AS-CODE|2|Define all infrastructure as version-controlled code; never provision or modify environments through manual steps.
CODE-AR-MESSAGE-BROKER|2|Route all inter-service messages through a central broker to decouple producers from consumers.
CODE-AR-PIPELINE-CHANGES|2|Apply all infrastructure changes through an automated pipeline; never run commands directly from a workstation.
CODE-AR-PIPES-AND-FILTERS|2|Decompose complex processing into independent single-responsibility filter stages connected by message channels.
CODE-AR-TEST-INFRASTRUCTURE|2|Test infrastructure code at every level — static analysis, unit, integration, and compliance — before applying it.
CODE-CC-AVOID-LOCKS-IN-HOT-PATHS|2|Hold locks only for the minimum time needed; never perform I/O or long operations inside a critical section.
CODE-CC-DOCUMENT-THREAD-SAFETY|2|Document every class's thread-safety guarantees explicitly as part of its public contract.
CODE-CC-HIGHER-LEVEL-CONCURRENCY|2|Use high-level concurrency utilities instead of raw wait/notify to avoid subtle synchronization bugs.
CODE-CC-PREFER-IMMUTABLE|2|Design shared objects to be immutable after construction so they require no synchronization to be thread-safe.
CODE-CC-SAFE-PUBLICATION|2|Never let `this` escape a constructor; publish object references to other threads only after construction completes.
CODE-CC-STRUCTURED-CONCURRENCY|2|Scope every concurrent task to a parent that awaits completion, propagates cancellation, and surfaces child errors.
CODE-CC-SYNC-SHARED-STATE|2|Guard every access to shared mutable data with a consistent synchronization mechanism.
CODE-CC-TASK-BASED-CONCURRENCY|2|Express concurrent work as tasks submitted to an executor; never manage threads directly.
CODE-CS-BOY-SCOUT|1|Always leave the code cleaner than you found it by making small improvements on every touch.
CODE-CS-BROKEN-WINDOWS|1|Fix quality lapses immediately; never let visible disorder signal that standards have dropped.
CODE-CS-CATCH-SPECIFIC-EXCEPTIONS|1|Always catch the most specific exception type; never catch root exception types silently.
CODE-CS-CIRCUIT-BREAKER|2|Wrap calls to external dependencies in a circuit breaker to stop cascading failures automatically.
CODE-CS-CQS|1|Every method must be either a command or a query, never both.
CODE-CS-DESIGN-BY-CONTRACT|2|Specify preconditions, postconditions, and invariants for every operation and enforce them explicitly.
CODE-CS-DRY|1|Every piece of knowledge must have a single, authoritative representation in the system.
CODE-CS-EXCEPTIONS-FOR-EXCEPTIONAL-CONDITIONS|1|Use exceptions only for genuinely exceptional conditions, never for ordinary control flow.
CODE-CS-FAIL-FAST|1|Detect errors as early as possible and report them clearly; never silently continue in a broken state.
CODE-CS-HYRUMS-LAW|2|Treat all observable behaviors as implicit API contract; any change may break a consumer.
CODE-CS-INFORMATION-HIDING|1|Hide design decisions behind module interfaces; expose only what callers must know.
CODE-CS-KISS|1|Choose the simplest solution that correctly solves the problem; never add unnecessary complexity.
CODE-CS-NIH|1|Prefer proven external solutions over building equivalent functionality in-house.
CODE-CS-NO-SILVER-BULLET|2|No single technique solves all problems; choose tools based on actual context and constraints.
CODE-CS-PARETO|2|Focus effort on the high-impact 20% of code paths, bugs, and features; deprioritize the rest.
CODE-CS-POSTELS-LAW|2|Be conservative in what you send and liberal in what you accept.
CODE-CS-TELL-DONT-ASK|1|Tell objects what to do; never ask for their data to make decisions externally.
CODE-CS-UNIFORM-ACCESS|2|Expose all module services through a uniform notation regardless of storage or computation.
CODE-CS-WET|1|Eliminate duplicated knowledge; never encode the same rule or policy in multiple places.
CODE-CS-YAGNI|2|Never add functionality until it is actually needed; implement only what current requirements demand.
CODE-DX-AFFORDANCES-AND-FEEDBACK|2|Every interactive element must signal what it does and confirm the effect of every action.
CODE-DX-CODE-FOR-READERS|1|Write code to be read by humans first; prioritize clarity over cleverness in every choice.
CODE-DX-DATA-INK-RATIO|2|Maximize the data-ink ratio; remove every visual element that doesn't convey information.
CODE-DX-DELETE-DEAD-CODE|1|Remove all code that is no longer executed or referenced; rely on version control for history.
CODE-DX-ERROR-PREVENTION|2|Design to prevent errors from occurring in the first place, not to recover from them after.
CODE-DX-NAMING|1|Every name must reveal intent; never use a name that requires a comment to explain it.
CODE-DX-RECOGNITION-OVER-RECALL|2|Make objects, actions, and options visible so users never need to recall them from memory.
CODE-DX-REDUCE-COGNITIVE-LOAD|2|Structure code so developers hold as few concepts in working memory as possible at any point.
CODE-DX-SMALL-FUNCTIONS|1|Keep functions small, doing one thing only; extract additional responsibilities into named functions.
CODE-DX-SYSTEM-STATUS-VISIBILITY|2|Always inform users what the system is doing through timely, appropriate feedback.
CODE-OB-ALERT-ON-SYMPTOMS|2|Alert on user-visible symptoms like error rates and latency SLOs, not on internal resource signals.
CODE-OB-DISTRIBUTED-TRACING|2|Assign a unique trace ID at every request entry point and propagate it through all services.
CODE-OB-EMIT-EVENTS|2|Emit rich contextual events for every unit of work rather than relying on pre-aggregated metrics.
CODE-OB-ERROR-BUDGET|2|Alert on SLO burn rate and treat reliability as highest priority when the error budget is exhausted.
CODE-OB-FOUR-GOLDEN-SIGNALS|2|Instrument every service to expose Latency, Traffic, Errors, and Saturation as the minimum signals.
CODE-OB-HEALTH-CHECK-API|2|Expose dedicated liveness and readiness endpoints on every service for orchestrators and load balancers.
CODE-OB-HIGH-CARDINALITY|2|Include high-cardinality fields like user IDs in telemetry to pinpoint exactly who and what is affected.
CODE-OB-PERCENTILE-LATENCY|2|Report tail percentiles (p99, p99.9) for latency; never rely on mean or maximum values alone.
CODE-OB-RED-METHOD|2|Instrument every service with Rate, Errors, and Duration as the minimum request-scoped signals.
CODE-OB-SERVICE-LEVEL-OBJECTIVES|2|Define explicit SLOs quantifying reliability targets and continuously measure performance against them.
CODE-OB-STRUCTURED-TELEMETRY|2|Emit structured, machine-parseable telemetry with request-scoped context on every event.
CODE-OB-USE-METHOD|2|For every resource, measure Utilisation, Saturation, and Errors to systematically detect bottlenecks.
CODE-PF-DATA-LOCALITY|3|Arrange data in memory so that items accessed together are stored contiguously for cache efficiency.
CODE-PF-MECHANICAL-SYMPATHY|3|Write code that works with hardware characteristics like cache lines and branch prediction, not against them.
CODE-PF-MINIMIZE-ALLOCATION|3|Minimize object creation in hot paths to reduce GC pressure and prevent unpredictable pauses.
CODE-PF-PREDICTABLE-LATENCY|3|Eliminate lock contention and GC pressure in hot paths to ensure consistent, predictable response times.
CODE-PF-PROFILE-FIRST|3|Always profile to identify actual bottlenecks before making any performance optimization.
CODE-RL-BACKPRESSURE|2|Signal producers to slow down when consumers are overwhelmed; never buffer unboundedly or drop silently.
CODE-RL-CONSISTENCY-MODELS|2|Choose consistency models explicitly per operation based on correctness requirements, not by accident.
CODE-RL-FAULT-TOLERANCE|2|Set timeouts on all remote calls and design for graceful degradation when any dependency fails.
CODE-RL-IDEMPOTENCY|2|Design operations so repeated execution produces the same result, enabling safe retries without side effects.
CODE-RL-SCHEMA-EVOLUTION|2|Design data schemas for forward and backward compatibility to enable independent service deployments.
CODE-SEC-COMPLETE-MEDIATION|2|Verify every access to every resource against the authorization policy on every request without exception.
CODE-SEC-DATA-INTEGRITY|2|Verify the integrity of all software artifacts, dependencies, and data before consuming them.
CODE-SEC-DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH|1|Layer independent security controls so that no single failure grants full access to a protected resource.
CODE-SEC-FAIL-SAFE-DEFAULTS|1|Deny access by default; any error or ambiguity in an authorization check must result in denial, never access.
CODE-SEC-PRIVACY-BY-DESIGN|2|Collect only required personal data, retain it minimally, and make privacy-protective behavior the default.
CODE-SEC-SECURITY-BY-DESIGN|2|Perform threat modeling and apply secure design patterns before implementation begins, not after.
CODE-SEC-SECURITY-LOGGING|2|Log all security-relevant events with full context and actively monitor for anomalous patterns and attacks.
CODE-SEC-STRONG-CRYPTOGRAPHY|2|Use only established cryptographic algorithms and libraries; never implement custom cryptographic primitives.
CODE-SEC-VALIDATE-INPUT|1|Validate all input at every system boundary; never trust external data.
CODE-SMELLS-ALTERNATIVE-CLASSES|2|Unify classes that perform the same role under a common interface to eliminate hidden duplication.
CODE-SMELLS-COMMENTS-AS-DEODORANT|2|Refactor code to make intent self-evident; use comments only to explain why, never what.
CODE-SMELLS-DATA-CLASS|2|Give data classes meaningful behavior; eliminate passive data holders that scatter logic into other classes.
CODE-SMELLS-DATA-CLUMPS|2|Extract recurring groups of related data fields into a named object rather than repeating them everywhere.
CODE-SMELLS-DIVERGENT-CHANGE|2|Split classes that change for multiple unrelated reasons to enforce single responsibility.
CODE-SMELLS-FEATURE-ENVY|2|Move methods that use another class's data more than their own into that other class.
CODE-SMELLS-GLOBAL-DATA|1|Eliminate global mutable state; restrict data access and modification to narrowly scoped locations.
CODE-SMELLS-INSIDER-TRADING|2|Decouple classes that access each other's private internals excessively by moving or hiding shared data.
CODE-SMELLS-LARGE-CLASS|2|Split classes with too many responsibilities into focused, single-purpose classes.
CODE-SMELLS-LAZY-ELEMENT|2|Remove or inline abstractions that do not justify their existence with meaningful logic or reuse.
CODE-SMELLS-LONG-METHOD|2|Extract logically distinct blocks from long methods into well-named, single-purpose methods.
CODE-SMELLS-LONG-PARAMETER-LIST|2|Replace long parameter lists with parameter objects to reduce complexity and prevent call-site errors.
CODE-SMELLS-LOOPS|2|Replace imperative loops that filter, transform, or accumulate with declarative pipeline operations.
CODE-SMELLS-MESSAGE-CHAINS|2|Break long navigation chains by moving logic closer to the data it needs, reducing coupling to structure.
CODE-SMELLS-MIDDLE-MAN|2|Remove or inline classes that do nothing but delegate to another class without adding meaningful logic.
CODE-SMELLS-MUTABLE-DATA|2|Minimize mutable state scope; prefer immutability and confine mutation to a single, explicit location.
CODE-SMELLS-PRIMITIVE-OBSESSION|2|Replace primitive types used as domain concepts with small, named domain objects that encapsulate behavior.
CODE-SMELLS-REFUSED-BEQUEST|2|Replace inheritance with composition when a subclass rejects significant portions of its parent's interface.
CODE-SMELLS-SHOTGUN-SURGERY|2|Consolidate scattered logic so that a single concept change requires editing only one location.
CODE-SMELLS-SPECULATIVE-GENERALITY|2|Build only for today's known requirements; remove unused abstractions and speculative dead flexibility.
CODE-SMELLS-SWITCH-STATEMENTS|2|Replace repeated switch/if-else type-dispatch chains with polymorphism so new variants require no edits.
CODE-SMELLS-TEMPORARY-FIELD|2|Never leave instance fields valid only sometimes; extract context-specific state into a dedicated class.
CODE-TP-BRANDED-TYPES|2|Wrap primitive values in distinct types so the compiler rejects mismatched domain concepts at compile time.
CODE-TP-EXHAUSTIVE-PATTERN-MATCHING|2|Use exhaustive pattern matching so the compiler enforces that every variant of a type is handled.
CODE-TP-MAKE-ILLEGAL-STATES-UNREPRESENTABLE|2|Design types so that invalid domain states cannot be constructed, not merely discouraged by convention.
CODE-TP-PREFER-SUM-TYPES|2|Model multi-variant values as sum types, not booleans, strings, or integer codes.
CODE-TP-TYPE-STATE-MACHINES|2|Encode distinct object states as separate types so invalid state transitions become compile-time errors.
CODE-TS-ARRANGE-ACT-ASSERT|2|Structure every test in three explicit phases: Arrange, Act, and Assert.
CODE-TS-CHARACTERIZATION-TESTS|2|Before refactoring legacy code, write characterization tests that pin its current behaviour as a safety net.
CODE-TS-CONSUMER-DRIVEN-CONTRACTS|2|Let consumers define the API contracts they need and verify providers satisfy all contracts in CI.
CODE-TS-FAST-TESTS|2|Keep tests fast enough to run after every small change without degrading the developer feedback loop.
CODE-TS-HUMBLE-OBJECT|2|Extract logic from framework-coupled classes into a dependency-free Humble Object that is easy to test.
CODE-TS-NO-TEST-LOGIC-IN-PRODUCTION|2|Never add test-only branches to production code; use dependency injection or seams instead.
CODE-TS-PROPERTY-BASED-TESTING|2|Specify invariants that must hold for any valid input and let the framework generate and shrink test cases.
CODE-TS-SINGLE-BEHAVIOR|2|Each test must assert exactly one logical behavior so failures pinpoint the broken condition immediately.
CODE-TS-TEST-BEHAVIOR|2|Test observable behavior through the public contract, never internal implementation details.
CODE-TS-TEST-DATA-BUILDER|2|Use the Builder pattern to construct test fixtures, setting only the fields relevant to each scenario.
CODE-TS-TEST-DOUBLES|2|Prefer real collaborators over test doubles; only mock external dependencies like networks and databases.
CODE-TS-TEST-FIRST|2|Write a failing test before any production code and follow the red-green-refactor cycle strictly.
CODE-TS-TEST-INDEPENDENCE|2|Each test must run in any order and in isolation, sharing no mutable state with other tests.
CODE-TS-TEST-NAMING|2|Name tests as behavior specifications that state context, action, and expected outcome without reading the body.
CODE-TS-TEST-PYRAMID|2|Build test suites as a pyramid: many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, minimal end-to-end tests.
CONFIG-CHANGE-TRACEABILITY|2|Every configuration change must flow through version control and peer review, producing a full audit trail.
CONFIG-ENVIRONMENT-PARITY|1|Keep config schema identical across all environments; vary only values, never structure or branching behaviour.
CONFIG-EXPLICIT-DEFAULTS|1|Declare every optional config key's default explicitly in the schema, not buried in application fallback logic.
CONFIG-EXPLICIT-OVER-CONVENTIONAL|1|Make every configuration key and its effect explicit; never rely on undocumented framework conventions.
CONFIG-MINIMAL-SURFACE|2|Remove all unused, deprecated, and duplicated configuration keys to minimise the configuration attack surface.
CONFIG-NO-HARDCODED-SECRETS|1|Never commit secrets to version control; supply them at runtime via dedicated secrets management tooling.
CONFIG-SCHEMA-FIRST|1|Define a schema declaring every config key before writing values; validate config files against it in CI.
CONFIG-SCHEMA-VALIDATION|1|Validate all configuration against a schema at startup and fail immediately on any missing or malformed value.
DB-ACID|1|Ensure every database transaction satisfies Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability guarantees.
DB-AVOID-N-PLUS-ONE|2|Never issue a separate query per row; replace N+1 patterns with joins, batch loads, or eager loading.
DB-CAP-THEOREM|2|Explicitly choose between consistency and availability during partitions before a distributed system is designed.
DB-CQRS|2|Separate command and query models when read and write paths have different scaling or structural requirements.
DB-DENORMALIZE-INTENTIONALLY|2|Denormalise only when a measured performance problem justifies it and the redundancy is explicitly managed.
DB-EVENT-SOURCING|2|Store the full sequence of immutable events rather than current state; derive state by replaying the log.
DB-EVENTUAL-CONSISTENCY|2|Design systems for eventual consistency explicitly; implement conflict detection and read guarantees where required.
DB-INDEX-FOR-ACCESS-PATTERNS|2|Create indexes based on specific measured query access patterns, not schema shape or general intuition.
DB-OPTIMISTIC-CONCURRENCY|2|Use optimistic concurrency in read-heavy workloads; detect conflicts at commit time and retry rather than locking.
DB-OUTBOX-PATTERN|2|Write events to an outbox table in the same transaction as domain changes to eliminate dual-write failures.
DB-POLYGLOT-PERSISTENCE|2|Choose the storage technology whose data model best fits each distinct access pattern in the system.
DB-SCHEMA-MIGRATIONS-AS-CODE|1|Express every schema change as a versioned migration file reviewed and applied exclusively by automated pipelines.
DB-THIRD-NORMAL-FORM|2|Design relational schemas to at least Third Normal Form by default; denormalise only when justified by measurement.
DB-TWO-PHASE-LOCKING|2|Acquire all locks before releasing any; keep transactions short to prevent deadlocks and serializability violations.
DDD-AGGREGATE-ROOT|2|Route all external access and modifications to an aggregate exclusively through its single root entity.
DDD-AGGREGATE|2|Group related entities and value objects into small consistency boundaries and enforce invariants within each transaction.
DDD-BOUNDED-CONTEXT|2|Define explicit model boundaries and map inter-context relationships rather than forcing a single unified model.
DDD-CONTEXT-MAP|2|Document explicit relationships between Bounded Contexts using a Context Map with named integration patterns.
DDD-DOMAIN-EVENT|2|Record meaningful domain occurrences as immutable past-tense events and publish them to decouple bounded contexts.
DDD-ENTITY|2|Define domain objects by unique identity, not attribute values, and base equality exclusively on that identity.
DDD-OPEN-HOST-SERVICE|2|Expose a versioned, documented protocol for external contexts to consume while keeping the internal model free to evolve.
DDD-PUBLISHED-LANGUAGE|2|Translate between bounded contexts using a shared, agreed-upon exchange format rather than exposing internal models.
DDD-REPOSITORY|2|Access and persist aggregates through a collection-like interface that hides all data-store implementation details.
DDD-SHARED-KERNEL|2|Share only a minimal, explicitly agreed-upon subset of the domain model and require bilateral consent for any change.
DDD-SPECIFICATION|2|Encapsulate business rules in named, combinable Specification objects with a single `isSatisfiedBy` method.
DDD-UBIQUITOUS-LANGUAGE|2|Use domain expert vocabulary exactly in all code, conversations, and documentation without translation.
DDD-VALUE-OBJECT|2|Model descriptive domain concepts without identity as immutable objects compared by full attribute equality.
DOC-ACCURACY|2|Keep documentation synchronized with the system; never let it describe behavior that no longer exists.
DOC-ADDRESSABLE|2|Give every meaningful document section a stable, direct link using consistent, non-duplicate headings.
DOC-AS-CODE|1|Store documentation in version control alongside code and apply the same review and CI workflow.
DOC-AUDIENCE|2|Write every document for a specific reader with a known background and a concrete goal in mind.
DOC-CLOSE-TO-CODE|1|Store documentation in the same directory as the artifact it describes to prevent drift.
DOC-DIAGRAMS-AS-CODE|1|Store diagrams as plain-text source files and render them at build or view time.
DOC-EXAMPLES|2|Accompany every abstract description with a concrete, runnable example that readers can try immediately.
DOC-MINIMAL|1|Omit speculative, derivable, or duplicated content; every sentence must earn its place by serving the reader.
DOC-OBJECTIVE|1|Use only factual, demonstrable claims and omit marketing adjectives like "easy", "simple", or "powerful".
DOC-PROGRESSIVE-DISCLOSURE|2|Lead with the simplest case and layer increasing complexity so readers can stop when they have enough.
DOC-PURPOSE|1|Assign each document exactly one purpose—tutorial, how-to, reference, or explanation—and never mix them.
DOC-SCANNABLE|1|Use descriptive headings, short paragraphs, and bold anchors so readers can locate information by scanning.
DOC-SELF-CONTAINED|2|Make every page intelligible in isolation by defining prerequisites and context without assuming prior reading.
DOC-TASK-ORIENTED|2|Structure documentation around user goals expressed as tasks, not around internal product components.
DOC-UNIQUE|1|Maintain a single canonical source for every piece of content and link to it rather than duplicating.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-BUILDER|2|Use the Builder pattern when constructors or factories require many parameters, especially optional ones.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-DESIGN-FOR-INHERITANCE|2|Document self-use of overridable methods and mark classes `final` unless explicitly designed for inheritance.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-DESIGN-INTERFACES-FOR-POSTERITY|2|Audit every default method added to an existing interface against all known implementations before release.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-DESIGN-METHOD-SIGNATURES|2|Keep parameter lists to four or fewer, use enums instead of booleans, and choose method names carefully.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-ELIMINATE-UNCHECKED-WARNINGS|2|Eliminate all unchecked warnings; suppress only when type-safety is proven and suppression is scoped as narrowly as possible.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-INTERFACES-FOR-TYPES|2|Use interfaces to define behavioral types; never use them solely as containers for constants.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-MINIMIZE-ACCESSIBILITY|2|Declare every class and member as inaccessible as possible to enforce encapsulation and hide implementation details.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-MINIMIZE-MUTABILITY|2|Make classes immutable by default: prevent extension, make fields final, and return new instances from operations.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-OVERRIDE-EQUALS-CONTRACT|1|Override `equals` only when fulfilling the full reflexive-symmetric-transitive contract, and always override `hashCode` too.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-PREFER-CLASS-HIERARCHIES|2|Replace tagged classes that branch on a type field with a proper abstract class hierarchy.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-PREFER-DEPENDENCY-INJECTION|1|Pass dependencies into constructors rather than hardwiring them as singletons or static utilities.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-PREFER-INTERFACES|2|Define types with interfaces rather than abstract classes; use abstract classes only for skeletal implementations.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-RETURN-EMPTY-COLLECTIONS|2|Return empty collections or zero-length arrays instead of `null` to eliminate mandatory null checks in callers.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-STATIC-FACTORY|2|Prefer static factory methods over constructors for named, cached, or polymorphic instance creation.
EFFECTIVE-JAVA-WRITE-DOC-COMMENTS|2|Document every exported API element with a Javadoc comment covering contract, parameters, return value, and exceptions.
EIP-AGGREGATOR|2|Always define a correlation expression, completeness condition, and timeout strategy for every Aggregator.
EIP-CLAIM-CHECK|2|Store large payloads externally and pass only a lightweight claim-check reference through the message bus.
EIP-CONTENT-BASED-ROUTER|2|Route messages to downstream channels based on content in a dedicated Router, never inside consumers.
EIP-CONTENT-ENRICHER|2|Enrich messages with additional data at a dedicated pipeline boundary before they reach downstream consumers.
EIP-CORRELATION-IDENTIFIER|2|Attach and propagate a unique Correlation Identifier through every message, downstream call, log, and trace span.
EIP-DEAD-LETTER-CHANNEL|2|Route undeliverable messages to a Dead Letter Channel for inspection and replay instead of discarding them.
EIP-IDEMPOTENT-CONSUMER|2|Track processed message IDs and discard duplicates to achieve effectively-once processing under at-least-once delivery.
EIP-MESSAGE-FILTER|2|Remove unwanted messages in a dedicated Filter component; never duplicate filtering logic in consumers.
EIP-MESSAGE-TRANSLATOR|2|Translate between message formats in a dedicated Translator so producers and consumers remain fully decoupled.
EIP-RETURN-ADDRESS|2|Include a Return Address in every request message so responders can route replies to the correct channel.
EIP-SPLITTER|2|Decompose composite messages into individual sub-messages and emit a correlation identifier on each one.
EIP-WIRE-TAP|2|Insert a Wire Tap to forward message copies to a secondary channel without affecting the primary flow.
FP-ALGEBRAIC-DATA-TYPES|2|Model domain concepts with product and sum types to make invalid states unrepresentable at compile time.
FP-AVOID-SHARED-MUTABLE-STATE|1|Eliminate shared mutable state; transform data through pipelines to prevent race conditions and action-at-a-distance bugs.
FP-CURRYING|2|Use currying and partial application to progressively specialise generic functions without duplication.
FP-EQUATIONAL-REASONING|2|Write pure, referentially transparent functions so program correctness can be verified by algebraic substitution.
FP-FUNCTION-COMPOSITION|2|Build complex behaviour by composing small, single-purpose functions where each output feeds the next input.
FP-FUNCTIONAL-CORE-IMPERATIVE-SHELL|2|Put all business logic in a pure functional core and confine I/O and side effects to a thin imperative shell.
FP-FUNCTOR-MONAD|2|Use functor and monad abstractions to compose operations over contextual types uniformly via map and flatMap.
FP-HIGHER-ORDER-FUNCTIONS|2|Treat functions as first-class values and abstract control structures via higher-order functions to eliminate boilerplate.
FP-IMMUTABILITY|1|Prefer immutable data structures; produce new values on transformation and make mutation the explicit exception.
FP-LAZY-EVALUATION|2|Defer computations until results are needed to decouple producers from consumers and enable infinite sequences.
FP-MONOIDS-SEMIGROUPS|2|Identify associative binary operations and identity elements in domain types to enable generic, parallelisable aggregation.
FP-OPTION-EITHER-TYPES|2|Use Option and Either types to make absence and failure explicit in signatures, forcing exhaustive handling by callers.
FP-PATTERN-MATCHING|2|Use exhaustive pattern matching on algebraic data types to eliminate missing-case bugs at compile time.
FP-PERSISTENT-DATA-STRUCTURES|2|Use persistent data structures with structural sharing to get immutability guarantees at O(log n) update cost.
FP-POINT-FREE-STYLE|2|Define functions by composing transformations without naming arguments to focus on the pipeline, not the data.
FP-PURE-FUNCTIONS|1|Write functions that always return the same output for the same input and cause no observable side effects.
FP-RECURSION|2|Express repetition through recursive functions with explicit base and recursive cases instead of mutable loop variables.
FP-REFERENTIAL-TRANSPARENCY|1|Write expressions that can be replaced by their evaluated values anywhere without changing program behaviour.
FP-TAIL-CALL-OPTIMISATION|2|Use tail-recursive calls or trampolining to achieve O(1) stack space for deep recursive computations.
FP-TOTALITY|2|Write total functions that return a declared output type for every input; never throw, loop forever, or return null.
GOF-ABSTRACT-FACTORY|2|Use Abstract Factory to create families of related objects without coupling client code to concrete classes.
GOF-ADAPTER|2|Use Adapter to convert an incompatible interface into one clients expect, enabling unrelated classes to cooperate.
GOF-BRIDGE|2|Decouple abstraction from implementation via composition so both can vary independently without subclass explosion.
GOF-BUILDER|2|Separate complex object construction from its representation using a Builder to support multiple representations.
GOF-CHAIN-OF-RESPONSIBILITY|2|Chain handlers and pass a request along the chain until one handles it, decoupling sender from receiver.
GOF-COMMAND|2|Encapsulate requests as Command objects to support parameterization, queuing, logging, and undoable operations.
GOF-COMPOSITE|2|Compose objects into tree structures so clients treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly.
GOF-COMPOSITION-OVER-INHERITANCE|2|Favour object composition over inheritance to keep classes focused and enable behaviour changes at runtime.
GOF-DECORATOR|2|Attach additional responsibilities to objects dynamically via Decorator instead of creating subclass combinations.
GOF-FACADE|2|Provide a unified, simplified interface to a subsystem so clients have a simple entry point to complex functionality.
GOF-FACTORY-METHOD|2|Define a factory method in the creator so subclasses decide which concrete class to instantiate.
GOF-FLYWEIGHT|2|Share intrinsic, immutable state across many fine-grained objects to eliminate memory-wasting duplication.
GOF-INTERPRETER|2|Map each grammar rule to a class and traverse the resulting tree to interpret domain-language expressions.
GOF-ITERATOR|2|Traverse collections through an iterator object; never expose the collection's internal structure to callers.
GOF-LAW-OF-DEMETER|1|Call methods only on direct collaborators; never chain through objects returned by other calls.
GOF-MEDIATOR|2|Route all inter-component communication through a mediator; components must never reference each other directly.
GOF-MEMENTO|2|Capture and restore object state through an opaque memento; never expose internal fields for undo or rollback.
GOF-NULL-OBJECT|2|Replace null references with a do-nothing object implementing the same interface to eliminate null checks.
GOF-OBSERVER|2|Notify observers automatically via subscription; never hard-code dependents inside the subject.
GOF-PROGRAM-TO-INTERFACE|2|Declare all variables and parameters against abstract interfaces, never against concrete implementation types.
GOF-PROTOTYPE|2|Create new objects by cloning a pre-configured prototype rather than constructing them from scratch.
GOF-PROXY|2|Use a same-interface surrogate to control, defer, or augment access to the real subject transparently.
GOF-SINGLETON|2|Prefer dependency injection of a shared instance over a global Singleton; avoid mutable global state.
GOF-STATE|2|Encapsulate state-specific behavior in separate state objects and delegate to the current state at runtime.
GOF-STRATEGY|2|Encapsulate each algorithm variant behind a common interface and inject the chosen strategy at runtime.
GOF-TEMPLATE-METHOD|2|Define the algorithm skeleton in a final base-class method; let subclasses override only the varying steps.
GOF-VISITOR|2|Add new operations to a stable object hierarchy via a new Visitor class; never modify the element classes.
GRASP-CONTROLLER|2|Route system events through a dedicated controller class; never embed coordination logic in UI or domain objects.
GRASP-CREATOR|2|Assign object-creation responsibility to the class that aggregates, contains, or holds initializing data for the new object.
GRASP-HIGH-COHESION|1|Keep each class focused on a single, closely related set of responsibilities; reject unrelated additions.
GRASP-INDIRECTION|2|Introduce an intermediary object to decouple two components so each can vary independently.
GRASP-INFORMATION-EXPERT|1|Assign each responsibility to the class that already holds the data needed to fulfill it.
GRASP-LOW-COUPLING|1|Depend on stable abstractions and minimise the number of classes each class directly references.
GRASP-POLYMORPHISM|2|Replace type-based conditionals with polymorphic dispatch; let each type handle its own variant behavior.
GRASP-PROTECTED-VARIATIONS|2|Wrap every predicted point of variation behind a stable interface so change is confined to one boundary.
GRASP-PURE-FABRICATION|2|Invent infrastructure classes with no domain equivalent to isolate technical concerns and keep domain objects clean.
INFRA-HIGH-AVAILABILITY|2|Deploy across independent failure domains and validate failover regularly against defined RTO and RPO targets.
INFRA-IMMUTABLE-INFRASTRUCTURE|2|Never modify running infrastructure; replace it by deploying a freshly built, versioned artifact from a pipeline.
INFRA-INFRASTRUCTURE-AS-CODE|1|Declare every infrastructure resource in version-controlled code and apply all changes through an automated pipeline.
INFRA-LEAST-PRIVILEGE|1|Grant every identity only the minimum permissions required for its function; no standing admin access.
INFRA-MINIMIZE-IMAGE-LAYERS|1|Use multi-stage builds and combine RUN instructions to minimize container image size and attack surface.
INFRA-NO-SECRETS-IN-CODE|1|Inject secrets at runtime from a secrets manager; never store credentials in code, config files, or logs.
INFRA-NO-SECRETS-IN-IMAGE|1|Never pass secrets via ENV, ARG, COPY, or ADD in Dockerfiles; use BuildKit secret mounts instead.
INFRA-NON-ROOT-CONTAINER|1|Every container must switch to a dedicated non-root user with the USER directive before runtime.
INFRA-OBSERVABLE-INFRASTRUCTURE|1|Emit structured, centralised telemetry from every infrastructure layer and alert on leading indicators.
INFRA-PIN-BASE-IMAGES|1|Pin every FROM instruction to a specific digest or full version tag; never use mutable tags like latest.
OWASP-01-BROKEN-ACCESS-CONTROL|2|Enforce server-side authorisation on every request; deny by default and verify resource ownership explicitly.
OWASP-02-CRYPTOGRAPHIC-FAILURES|3|Use strong, current cryptographic algorithms with managed keys for all sensitive data in transit and at rest.
OWASP-03-INJECTION|3|Use parameterised queries and structured APIs for all interpreter calls; never concatenate user data into commands.
OWASP-04-INSECURE-DESIGN|2|Threat-model every feature before implementation and embed security controls at the architectural design level.
OWASP-05-SECURITY-MISCONFIGURATION|2|Change defaults, disable unused features, and enforce hardening checklists across all components and environments.
OWASP-06-VULNERABLE-COMPONENTS|2|Track all dependency versions, run SCA in CI, and fail builds on high-severity known vulnerabilities.
OWASP-07-AUTHENTICATION-FAILURES|3|Use MFA, rate-limit login endpoints, use vetted auth libraries, and invalidate sessions on logout.
OWASP-08-SOFTWARE-INTEGRITY-FAILURES|2|Verify checksums and signatures for all artifacts; never deserialize untrusted data into complex objects.
OWASP-09-LOGGING-FAILURES|2|Log all security-relevant events with full context, aggregate centrally, and alert on anomalous patterns.
OWASP-10-SSRF|2|Validate all server-side request URLs against a strict allowlist of permitted schemes, hosts, and ports.
PIPELINE-DEPLOYMENT-GATES|2|Require explicit approval or automated policy checks before every deployment to a protected environment.
PIPELINE-ENVIRONMENT-ISOLATION|1|Run each pipeline stage in an isolated context with explicit artifact passing and per-stage credentials.
PIPELINE-FAIL-FAST-PIPELINE|1|Abort the pipeline on the first stage failure; never suppress errors in build, test, or deploy steps.
PIPELINE-MINIMAL-PERMISSIONS|1|Grant each pipeline job only the minimum permissions and secrets required for its specific task.
PIPELINE-NO-SECRETS-IN-LOGS|1|Never echo secrets in pipeline output; use masked variables and pass credentials via stdin or credential helpers.
PIPELINE-REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS|1|Pin all dependency versions and build tool images so identical inputs always produce identical artifacts.
PKG-ADP|1|Eliminate all cycles in the component dependency graph to enable independent builds and releases.
PKG-CCP|2|Group into one component only the classes that change at the same time for the same reasons.
PKG-CRP|2|Never bundle classes into a component unless all consumers of the component use all of them together.
PKG-REP|2|Group into a component only classes cohesive enough to be released, versioned, and maintained together.
PKG-SAP|2|Stable components must consist primarily of abstract types so they remain extensible without modification.
PKG-SDP|2|Depend only on components more stable than yourself; keep volatile components at the system's edges.
PTAC-COMPOSABLE-FILES|1|Keep files small, focused, and composable rather than large and monolithic.
PTAC-LEAN-CONTENT|1|Keep plain-text content DRY and front-loaded; every line must earn its place.
PTAC-NAVIGABLE-DIRS|2|Every plain-text directory must contain an INDEX.md listing its contents and a README.md describing its purpose.
PTAC-NO-GENERATED-BLOBS|2|Commit source and generation instructions, not generated or binary output blobs.
PTAC-PLAIN-TEXT-FIRST|1|Prefer plain text formats over binary; keep every artifact diffable and human-readable.
SCHEMA-ENUM-EVOLUTION|1|Every enum must include an UNSPECIFIED sentinel and all consumers must handle unknown values without crashing.
SCHEMA-FIELD-OPTIONALITY|1|Every new schema field must be optional with a sensible default; never add required fields to published schemas.
SCHEMA-NO-POLYMORPHIC-BLOBS|1|Never use untyped blobs or Any fields; define an explicit, validated schema for every structured payload.
SCHEMA-SELF-DESCRIBING|1|Every field, enum value, and method must be described clearly enough to understand without external documentation.
SEC-ARCH-ECONOMY-OF-MECHANISM|2|Keep security mechanisms as small and simple as possible; use established libraries, never custom cryptography.
SEC-ARCH-LEAST-COMMON-MECHANISM|2|Minimize shared credentials, caches, and mechanisms between users or components at different trust levels.
SEC-ARCH-OPEN-DESIGN|2|Design security to remain strong when fully disclosed; derive it from key secrecy, never from obscurity.
SEC-ARCH-PSYCHOLOGICAL-ACCEPTABILITY|2|Make the secure path the easiest path; security controls must never incentivize workarounds.
SEC-ARCH-SEPARATION-OF-PRIVILEGE|2|Require two or more independent conditions to grant access to any sensitive resource or operation.
SEC-ARCH-SUPPLY-CHAIN-SECURITY|2|Verify artifact provenance by digest, generate SBOMs, sign releases, and use hermetic build pipelines.
SEC-ARCH-THREAT-MODELLING|2|Enumerate threats with STRIDE before implementation and document every mitigation in version-controlled artifacts.
SEC-ARCH-ZERO-TRUST|2|Authenticate and authorize every request explicitly; never grant implicit trust based on network location.
SIMPLE-DESIGN-FEWEST-ELEMENTS|1|Include the fewest classes and abstractions possible once tests pass, intent is clear, and duplication is removed.
SIMPLE-DESIGN-NO-DUPLICATION|1|Every piece of business logic must exist in exactly one place; eliminate all knowledge duplication.
SIMPLE-DESIGN-PASSES-TESTS|1|All code must pass every test before any other design quality concern is addressed.
SIMPLE-DESIGN-REVEALS-INTENTION|1|Every name and structure must reveal its purpose so that readers need not decipher the code.
SOLID-DIP|1|High-level modules must depend on abstractions, never on concrete low-level implementations.
SOLID-ISP|1|Split fat interfaces into focused role interfaces so each client depends only on methods it actually uses.
SOLID-LSP|1|Subtypes must be fully substitutable for their base type without requiring special-case logic in callers.
SOLID-OCP|1|Add new behavior by writing new code that implements an abstraction; never modify existing proven code.
SOLID-SRP|1|Each module or class must have exactly one reason to change, serving a single stakeholder or concern.
